sources.notes: Maintain changing attack/defense values and answer each update with the predicted popularity count.
Official reference code converts every item into a demand value, applies range additions over thresholds, and finds the first negative segment-tree leaf.

annotation.summary: After point updates to two attributes, maintain the maximum predicted popularity value under global attack and defense baselines.
annotation.constraints: n and the number of updates are large, so each update needs logarithmic maintenance of threshold counts.
annotation.core_idea: For each object, compute its demand p=max(a_i-A,0)+max(d_i-D,0): how much extra resource it needs before it can count toward a popularity level. A level x is feasible exactly when enough objects have demand below x. Maintain for every x the value count(demand<x)-x; each object with demand p contributes +1 to all thresholds x>p. A lazy segment tree supports removing and adding one object's range contribution and finds the first negative threshold, so the answer is that threshold minus one.
tricks: convert each item to a demand threshold

solution_variants.name: threshold-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Maintain demand-threshold contributions with range add and query the first level whose count is insufficient.

tags.evidence: Range add over threshold suffixes and first-negative search are the core data structure operations.
Each object contributes +1 to a suffix of popularity thresholds, and updates are handled by removing and re-adding that contribution.
The answer is found as the first threshold where the monotone feasibility count becomes insufficient.
